name: Graph-Coloring Register Allocation
summary: Chaitin (1981) formulated register allocation as graph coloring: nodes are live ranges, edges connect simultaneously-live ranges, K colors map to physical registers. Produces excellent code at NP-complete cost; production GCC IRA/LRA descend from this lineage.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Chaitin (1981) formulated register allocation as graph coloring: build an interference graph where nodes are live ranges and edges connect simultaneously-live ranges, then color the graph with K colors (K = number of physical registers). If a node cannot be colored, it is *spilled* to memory — its value is loaded and stored at each use, freeing the register.

Graph coloring produces excellent code but is expensive: the interference graph can be quadratic in the number of live ranges, and coloring is NP-complete in general (though heuristics work well in practice). Chaitin's original algorithm and its descendants — Briggs's improvements, iterated register coalescing, optimistic coloring — set the upper bound on what register allocators can achieve.

sections.title: Chaitin's algorithm
sections.content:
The classical formulation has four phases. **Build**: compute liveness across the program, then construct the interference graph by connecting any two live ranges whose live intervals overlap. **Coalesce**: merge live ranges connected by a copy if their interference graphs do not conflict, eliminating the copy. **Simplify and select**: repeatedly remove a node of degree less than K from the graph (it can always be colored after the rest is colored), pushing it onto a stack; pop nodes off the stack and assign each the lowest-numbered color not used by its still-present neighbors. **Spill**: if a node has degree ≥ K and cannot be simplified, choose it as a spill candidate, rebuild, and retry.

Briggs's improvement is **optimistic coloring**: even nodes with degree ≥ K are pushed onto the stack with a flag, and during select they are tried for coloring; many succeed because not all neighbors actually use distinct colors. The classical Briggs spill heuristic is `cost / degree`, biasing toward cheap-to-spill, high-pressure live ranges.

sections.title: Production usage
sections.content:
GCC's IRA/LRA (integrated register allocator / local register allocator) lineage uses graph-coloring ideas, with substantial engineering for spill-heuristic tuning, region-based allocation to bound graph sizes, and careful handling of register classes that interfere asymmetrically (e.g., x86 partial-register stalls).

LLVM's production allocators are better described as live-interval, greedy, splitting, and linear-scan-influenced rather than classical Chaitin-style graph coloring. The classical algorithm appears mostly in academic systems and a few specialized backends; the closer descendants in production are SSA-based allocators (Pereira-Palsberg, regalloc2) that exploit SSA's lifetime-splitting properties to avoid the worst Chaitin pathologies.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.content:
Graph-coloring produces near-optimal allocation when the heuristics align with the workload. The cost is compile time: building the interference graph is O(n²) in the worst case, and the spill loop can iterate many times on register-pressured code. For optimising AOT compilation the trade-off is acceptable; for JIT compilation it is usually too slow.

The second cost is implementation complexity. A correct graph-coloring allocator that handles real ABIs (callee-saved registers, register classes, calling conventions, two-address instructions, register pairs) is among the hardest single passes in a backend. Most modern compilers have moved toward simpler allocators with more sophisticated live-range splitting (greedy, SSA-aware, Pereira-Palsberg).
